hero.headline: How does Michigan state income tax work for employers in 2026?
hero.subhead: Flat 4.25%, $9,000 UI wage base, new tip and overtime exemptions. Three things most payroll guides haven't updated yet.

sections.question: What is Michigan's state income tax rate for employers in 2026?
sections.answer_first: Michigan taxes all individual income at a flat 4.25% in 2026. There are no brackets. The personal exemption is $5,900 per allowance (up from $5,800 in 2025).
sections.body:
Michigan's flat-rate structure makes withholding calculation straightforward compared to progressive-bracket states. You multiply taxable wages by 4.25% after subtracting the value of exemptions claimed on Form MI-W4. A single employee with no dependents claims one exemption ($5,900), reducing their annual taxable income by that amount before the 4.25% applies.

The 2026 personal exemption of $5,900 was confirmed by Michigan Treasury in April 2026. Employees should update their MI-W4 if life circumstances changed — a new dependent or marriage changes the exemption count and the withholding calculation.

Michigan's flat rate has held at 4.25% since 2016 (temporarily reduced to 4.05% for 2023 under the state's revenue trigger mechanism, then restored to 4.25% for 2024 and maintained through 2026). There is no separate state standard deduction.

Employers file withholding returns and remit through Michigan's combined SUW (Sales, Use and Withholding) system. Annual reconciliation uses Form 5081, due 28th February.

sources.text: Michigan Department of Treasury 2026 Withholding Guide (446 Rev. 02-26)

sections.question: Are tip income and overtime pay exempt from Michigan income tax in 2026?
sections.answer_first: Yes — for 2026, 2027, and 2028. Michigan temporarily exempts tip income and overtime pay from the 4.25% personal income tax under new legislation effective 1st January 2026.
sections.body:
Michigan's Tipped and Hourly Workers Act created a temporary income tax exemption for two categories of wages. Tip income (amounts received from customers as gratuities) and overtime pay (hours above 40 per week at 1.5× rate) are excluded from Michigan taxable income for the three-year window 2026–2028.

For employers, this means two practical payroll changes. First, tipped employees need less Michigan state withholding withheld against their tip amounts — you separate tip income from regular wages in your payroll system and reduce withholding accordingly. Second, overtime hours must be flagged separately so the overtime premium is excluded from Michigan taxable wages. The base regular-rate earnings for overtime hours remain taxable; only the premium (the 0.5× uplift) is exempt.

The exemptions expire after the 2028 calendar year unless the Michigan legislature extends them. Build that sunset into any long-term cost modelling for tipped or overtime-heavy workforces.

A named Teamed payroll specialist reviews withholding setup at onboarding and runs compliance checks when Michigan law changes. The MiUI system update already reflects the 2026 exemptions — you don't build the workaround yourself.

sources.text: Honigman LLP — New Compliance Requirements Under Michigan's Temporary Tax Relief for Tipped and Hourly Workers

sections.question: How does Michigan employer withholding work?
sections.answer_first: Michigan employers withhold state income tax at a flat 4.25% on each paycheck, using the employee's MI-W4. Payment frequency — monthly, quarterly, or accelerated — depends on your liability size.
sections.body:
The withholding process starts with Form MI-W4. Every new Michigan employee submits this before their first paycheck. MI-W4 captures filing status and the number of exemptions claimed. Without a completed MI-W4 on file, Michigan requires withholding at the single/zero exemptions rate, which over-withholds for most employees and creates reconciliation friction at year-end.

Michigan assigns employers to payment frequency tiers based on prior-year liability:

- **Annual filers** (under $250 total annual liability): remit once, by 31st January
- **Quarterly filers** (under $700 per quarter): remit by the 20th of the month after each quarter-end
- **Monthly filers** (over $700 in prior quarter): remit by the 20th of the following month
- **Accelerated filers** ($40,000+ prior-year withholding): remit within 3 business days of each pay date; EFT required

Annual reconciliation uses Form 5081, due 28th February. Employers filing 250 or more W-2s must file electronically. Detroit city income tax (2.4% residents, 1.2% non-residents) requires separate registration and filings through Detroit's own treasury system.

Teamed's platform monitors your Michigan withholding obligations automatically — payment deadlines tracked, filing frequency reviewed annually as your headcount grows, MI-W4 changes picked up in real time. One platform, one invoice for all statutory costs itemised at cost.

sources.text: Michigan Department of Treasury — Withholding Tax Information by Calendar Year

sections.question: What is Michigan's unemployment insurance tax rate in 2026?
sections.answer_first: Michigan UI rates run 0.06%–12.2% in 2026, up from a 0.06%–10.3% range in 2025. New employers pay 2.7%. The taxable wage base is $9,000 per employee per year.
sections.body:
Michigan's UI tax is one of the most experience-rated systems in the United States, meaning your individual rate closely tracks actual benefit charges against your account. The 2026 rate increase at the top end (12.2% vs 10.3%) reflects the state's response to pandemic-era trust fund drawdowns.

Your specific rate is built from three components: the Chargeable Benefits Component (CBC, based on your claims history), the Account Building Component (ABC, a credit balance mechanism), and the Nonchargeable Benefits Component (NBC, a shared assessment). The UIA sends Form UIA 1771 each January with your computed rate for the coming year. You have 30 days to challenge it.

| Employer type | 2026 UI rate | Wage base | Max tax/employee |  
|---|---|---|---|
| New employer (general) | 2.7% | $9,000 | $243/year |
| New construction employer | 5.0% | $9,000 | $450/year |
| Experienced (lowest) | 0.06% | $9,000 | $5.40/year |
| Experienced (highest) | 12.2% | $9,000 | $1,098/year |
| Delinquent employer | Rate + $500 wage base | $9,500 | Higher |

Michigan is not a FUTA credit-reduction state, so the net federal FUTA rate remains 0.6% on the first $7,000 per employee per year.

sources.text: Bloomberg Tax — Michigan Increases Unemployment Insurance Tax Rates for 2026
EY Tax News — 2026 State Unemployment Insurance Taxable Wage Bases

sections.question: How do I register for Michigan unemployment insurance as an employer?
sections.answer_first: New Michigan employers register through MiUI, the UIA's new employer portal launched December 2025, which replaced the legacy MiWAM system. You'll need your Federal Employer Identification Number (FEIN).
sections.body:
Michigan completed a major UI portal migration in December 2025, replacing MiWAM with MiUI for employer tax functions. If you search for 'Michigan UI employer login' and land on MiWAM, you're on the legacy system — the UIA still maintains MiWAM for some claimant-side functions, but employer tax filings now go through MiUI.

The Q1 2026 transition caused disruption: the UIA granted a one-time penalty waiver for employers who missed the April 25, 2026 quarterly deadline, extending the deadline to 31st May 2026. If you're setting up Michigan payroll now, file through MiUI from the start.

Quarterly UI wage and tax reports use Form UIA 1028, due by the 25th of the month following each quarter-end:

- Q1 (January–March): due April 25
- Q2 (April–June): due July 25
- Q3 (July–September): due October 25
- Q4 (October–December): due January 25

Late filing penalties are $50 for the first quarter and $250 for each subsequent quarter.

Teamed's US payroll partner (Suna Solutions) handles UI registration, quarterly UIA 1028 filings, and MiUI account maintenance for every Michigan employee on an EOR arrangement. Use the EOR vs Entity Crossover Calculator to model when your own Michigan entity makes financial sense — typically between 8 and 12 employees in the state.

sources.text: Small Business Association of Michigan — Unemployment Insurance Changes in Michigan 2026

sections.question: Does Michigan require paid sick leave in 2026?
sections.answer_first: Yes. Michigan's Earned Sick Time Act (ESTA), which took effect 21st February 2025, requires all private employers to provide paid sick time. Large employers (11+ employees) must provide up to 72 hours; small employers up to 40 hours.
sections.body:
The ESTA replaced the narrower Paid Medical Leave Act (PMLA) and extends paid sick leave to all employees, not just those of large employers. Employees accrue sick time at one hour per 30 hours worked, starting from their date of hire (or the ESTA effective date for existing employees).

New employees may be required to wait up to 120 calendar days before using accrued hours, but the accrual starts on day one. Employers may also frontload the full annual amount at the start of the benefit year in lieu of the accrual method.

The ESTA covers a broad range of qualifying reasons: illness, medical appointments, domestic violence leave, and care for qualifying family members. It does not create a state paid family leave entitlement — Michigan has no state PFML programme. For family or medical leave beyond sick time, employees rely on federal FMLA (unpaid, 12 weeks; applies only to employers with 50+ employees).

Teamed tracks ESTA carryover caps and qualifying reasons for every Michigan employee, flagged in real time on the platform. No spreadsheet maintenance on your side.

sources.text: Paychex — Michigan Earned Sick Time Act: Rules for 2025
Burr & Forman — Michigan's Earned Sick Time Act, With Amendments, Has Gone Into Effect

sections.question: What should you know before running Michigan payroll in 2026?
sections.body:
Michigan's flat income tax is simpler than most US states — but 2026 added two wrinkles that matter operationally. The temporary tip and overtime exemptions require payroll system changes before you can withhold correctly. And the MiUI platform transition means any UI account setup or Q1 2026 filing done through the old MiWAM portal may need to be migrated.

Detroit is the most common city-tax issue: if any of your Michigan employees live in Detroit, you need a separate Detroit withholding account and must use Detroit's own withholding tables (2.4% for residents). Grand Rapids, Lansing, and around 20 other cities also have local taxes — always confirm residence at onboarding.

Michigan is an at-will state. Final paychecks are due on the next regular payday after termination (MCL 408.475) — there's no shorter window triggered by the method of separation. Michigan has no state PFML, which makes it a lower employer-cost state for leave obligations compared to New York, California, or Washington.

Teamed runs Michigan EOR through Suna Solutions. As of 30th May 2026, Teamed has not signalled plans to establish a Michigan-based Teamed entity; the EOR model through Suna remains the standard path.

faq_schema.q: What is Michigan's state income tax rate in 2026?
faq_schema.a: Michigan taxes all individual income at a flat 4.25% in 2026. There are no progressive brackets. The personal exemption is $5,900 per qualifying allowance claimed on Form MI-W4. New for 2026 through 2028: tip income and overtime pay are temporarily exempt from Michigan income tax.

faq_schema.q: What is the Michigan unemployment insurance tax rate in 2026?
faq_schema.a: Michigan UI rates run 0.06% to 12.2% for experienced employers in 2026, up from a 10.3% maximum in 2025. New employers pay 2.7% (construction: 5%). The taxable wage base is $9,000 per employee per year, making the maximum annual UI cost $1,098 per experienced employee at the top rate.

faq_schema.q: What is the MiUI portal in Michigan?
faq_schema.a: MiUI is Michigan's new unemployment insurance employer portal, launched December 2025 to replace the legacy MiWAM system. Employers use MiUI to register for UI, file quarterly UIA 1028 wage reports, and pay UI contributions. Quarterly reports are due on the 25th of the month after each quarter-end.

faq_schema.q: Does Michigan have paid family leave in 2026?
faq_schema.a: No. Michigan does not operate a state paid family and medical leave (PFML) programme. Michigan employers do need to comply with the Earned Sick Time Act (ESTA, effective 21 February 2025), which requires paid sick time for all employees: 72 hours per year for employers with 11 or more employees, 40 hours for smaller employers. Federal FMLA (unpaid, 12 weeks) applies to employers with 50 or more employees.

faq_schema.q: Does Michigan have a local income tax?
faq_schema.a: Yes. Approximately 24 Michigan cities impose local income taxes, with Detroit charging the highest rates: 2.4% for residents and 1.2% for non-residents working in Detroit. Grand Rapids, Lansing, Flint, and approximately 20 other cities also levy local income taxes. Employers must withhold city taxes separately from state income tax.

meta.title: Michigan State Income Tax and Unemployment Insurance 2026 | Teamed
meta.description: Michigan employer withholding 2026: flat 4.25%, $5,900 exemption, new tip/overtime exemption. UI rates 0.06%–12.2%, $9,000 wage base. EOR via Suna Solutions at $599/month.
